chunks.text: Julian Knight MP Chair, DCMS Select Committee 20 June 2022 BY EMAIL Dear Julian Thank you for the invitation to give evidence to your Committee on 7 th June with regard to the Online Safety Bill. We spoke at length about the powers that are granted to the Secretary of State in the Bill, a concern shared by my fellow panellists. As I said in my evidence: Oneofthebest measures to protect freedom of expression in the Bill would be torowback a little on some of the powers of the Secretary of State, of the Executive, to interfere in what the independent arm’s length regulator does and the decisions it takes in its regulation because the underpinning convention of regulation of media in Western Europe is that there is an independent regulator and the Executive does not interfere in their daytoday decision-making forverygood reason. In the regime setouthere, there arefewtoomany powers for the Secretary of State to interfere in that manner, rather than merely giving strategic direction on important matters. I understand from a follow-up conversation with the Committee’s advisor that you may be thinking further about recommending amendments in this are a to the Public Bill Committee. I hope that the following note, which sets out the arguments for – and detail of – amendments to clauses 40, 147 and 146 will be helpful. Background The Secretary of State should not be a bletogive OFCOM specific direction on non-strategic matters. OFCOM’s independence in day-to-day decision making is paramount to preserving freedom of expression. Independence of media regulators is the norm in developed democracies and the UK has signed uptomany international statements in this vein, as recently as April 2022 at the Council of Europe. ‘media and communication governance should be independent and impartial to avoid undue influence on policy making, discriminatory treatment and preferential treatment of powerful groups, including those with significant political or economic power’ The Billhas introduced powers for the SoS to direct OFCOM on internet safety codes – these should be removed. After all, in broadcasting regulation OFCOM is trusted to make powerful programme codes with no interference from the SoS. The SoS takes powers to give tactical guidance to OFCOM on the ‘exercise of their functions’ as well as strategic guidance: the tactical guidance should be removed. The Bill also expands unconstrained emergency-like powers in the draft Bill that would allow the SoS to direct OFCOM and even target individual companies – the ability to direct should be removed to preserve OFCOM’s independence. 1 - Clause 40 Secretary of State’s powers of direction
chunks.display_text: Julian Knight MP Chair, DCMS Select Committee 20 June 2022 BY EMAIL Dear Julian Thank you for the invitation to give evidence to your Committee on 7 th June with regard to the Online Safety Bill. We spoke at length about the powers that are granted to the Secretary of State in the Bill, a concern shared by my fellow panellists. As I said in my evidence: Oneofthebest measures to protect freedom of expression in the Bill would be torowback a little on some of the powers of the Secretary of State, of the Executive, to interfere in what the independent arm’s length regulator does and the decisions it takes in its regulation because the underpinning convention of regulation of media in Western Europe is that there is an independent regulator and the Executive does not interfere in their daytoday decision-making forverygood reason. In the regime setouthere, there arefewtoomany powers for the Secretary of State to interfere in that manner, rather than merely giving strategic direction on important matters. I understand from a follow-up conversation with the Committee’s advisor that you may be thinking further about recommending amendments in this are a to the Public Bill Committee. I hope that the following note, which sets out the arguments for – and detail of – amendments to clauses 40, 147 and 146 will be helpful. Background The Secretary of State should not be a bletogive OFCOM specific direction on non-strategic matters. OFCOM’s independence in day-to-day decision making is paramount to preserving freedom of expression. Independence of media regulators is the norm in developed democracies and the UK has signed uptomany international statements in this vein, as recently as April 2022 at the Council of Europe. ‘media and communication governance should be independent and impartial to avoid undue influence on policy making, discriminatory treatment and preferential treatment of powerful groups, including those with significant political or economic power’ The Billhas introduced powers for the SoS to direct OFCOM on internet safety codes – these should be removed. After all, in broadcasting regulation OFCOM is trusted to make powerful programme codes with no interference from the SoS. The SoS takes powers to give tactical guidance to OFCOM on the ‘exercise of their functions’ as well as strategic guidance: the tactical guidance should be removed. The Bill also expands unconstrained emergency-like powers in the draft Bill that would allow the SoS to direct OFCOM and even target individual companies – the ability to direct should be removed to preserve OFCOM’s independence. 1 - Clause 40 Secretary of State’s powers of direction

chunks.header: Clause 40(1)(a) gives the Secretary of State power to direct OFCOM to make
chunks.text: specific changes to some of OFCOM’s draft codes of practice if they ‘ believes that…modifications are required…for reasons of public policy ’. The codes are the fulcrum of the regulatory regime and this is a significant interference in OFCOM’s independence. OFCOM itself has noted that the ‘reasons of public policy’ power to direct might weaken the regime. If OFCOM has undertaken a logical process rooted in evidence to arrive at a draft code thenitishard toseehow a ‘reasons of public policy’ based direction is not irrational. This then creates a vulnerability to legal challenge. The same clause gives the SoS powers to direct OFCOM on national security or public safety grounds the Terrorism and CSEA codes of practice. The SoS might have some special knowledge on these but the government has not demonstrated why it needs a power to direct. In the broadcasting regime, there are no equivalent powers and the SoS was able to resolve the case of “Russia Today” on national security grounds with public correspondence between the SoS and OFCOM. As I said to the Committee, Clause 40 is “ explicitly drafted so that the Secretary of State can infinitely reject proposals it receives from Ofcom until it gets the proposal it wants. It is a very unusual power and that is before the Secretary of State then brings the SI to Parliament … If Ofcom's work is based on its enormous research and evidential capability, I as a former civil servant who worked in DCMS, frankly cannot see how DCMS can surpass that on an infinite number of occasions in any manner that is other than irrational.” The code signals a willingness of government to wear OFCOM down and impose the government’s view on the regulator. It seems to be preparing the ground for an irrational or highly disputed request. Amendment We suggest replacing Clause 40 with the ability for the Secretary of State to write to OFCOM in public with observations on codes and for OFCOM to have regard to such letters. This is the conventional approach to UK government/regulator relationships. This gives the Secretary of State a privileged position in national consultation, allowing them to critique the final version of a code (a second bite – OFCOM must consult the government on a draft) and in a manner that also slows the process slightly. The government’s ability to write letters should not be infinite. Codes issued after such correspondence should be a pproved by the House using the affirmative procedure (Clause 41). Amending in this fashion greatly simplifies the process. Detailed Amendments
chunks.display_text: specific changes to some of OFCOM’s draft codes of practice if they ‘ believes that…modifications are required…for reasons of public policy ’. The codes are the fulcrum of the regulatory regime and this is a significant interference in OFCOM’s independence. OFCOM itself has noted that the ‘reasons of public policy’ power to direct might weaken the regime. If OFCOM has undertaken a logical process rooted in evidence to arrive at a draft code thenitishard toseehow a ‘reasons of public policy’ based direction is not irrational. This then creates a vulnerability to legal challenge. The same clause gives the SoS powers to direct OFCOM on national security or public safety grounds the Terrorism and CSEA codes of practice. The SoS might have some special knowledge on these but the government has not demonstrated why it needs a power to direct. In the broadcasting regime, there are no equivalent powers and the SoS was able to resolve the case of “Russia Today” on national security grounds with public correspondence between the SoS and OFCOM. As I said to the Committee, Clause 40 is “ explicitly drafted so that the Secretary of State can infinitely reject proposals it receives from Ofcom until it gets the proposal it wants. It is a very unusual power and that is before the Secretary of State then brings the SI to Parliament … If Ofcom's work is based on its enormous research and evidential capability, I as a former civil servant who worked in DCMS, frankly cannot see how DCMS can surpass that on an infinite number of occasions in any manner that is other than irrational.” The code signals a willingness of government to wear OFCOM down and impose the government’s view on the regulator. It seems to be preparing the ground for an irrational or highly disputed request. Amendment We suggest replacing Clause 40 with the ability for the Secretary of State to write to OFCOM in public with observations on codes and for OFCOM to have regard to such letters. This is the conventional approach to UK government/regulator relationships. This gives the Secretary of State a privileged position in national consultation, allowing them to critique the final version of a code (a second bite – OFCOM must consult the government on a draft) and in a manner that also slows the process slightly. The government’s ability to write letters should not be infinite. Codes issued after such correspondence should be a pproved by the House using the affirmative procedure (Clause 41). Amending in this fashion greatly simplifies the process. Detailed Amendments

chunks.header: Clause 40 amend title to –‘Secretary of State’s powers to suggest modification to (Part 1/2)
chunks.text: a code of practice’ CL 40(1) delete after ‘may’ to end. Insert after ‘may’: ‘on receipt of a code write with in one month of that day to OFCOM with reasoned, evidence-based suggestions for modifying the code. (a) OFCOM shall have due regard to the Secretary of State’s letter. OFCOM shall reply to the Secretary of State with in one month of receipt. (b) The Secretary of State may only write to OFCOM twice under this Section foreachcode. (c) The Secretary of State and OFCOM shall publish their letters as soon as reasonable possible after transmission, having made any reasonable redactions for public safety and national security. Cl 40(2)- (12) delete Cl 41 title of Clause – replace ‘direction’ with ‘changes arising from correspondence under Section 40’ 41(1) delete 41(2) replace text after ‘following a’ with ‘changes to a code of practice arising from correspondence under Section 40, the affirmative procedure applies’. 41(3) delete 41(5)-(7) delete Note that, based on our advice, the Shadow Digital Minister tabled a similar amendment (NC12) which was debated by the Committee but rejected by the Government on 14 th June . 2 – Clause 147 Secretary of State’s Guidance The Secretary of State takes powers in Cl147 to issue tactical guidance to OFCOM on the ‘exercise of their functions’, to which OFCOM should have regard. This is in addition to Cl 143 which allows the SoS to make a statement of strategic priorities relating to online safety. The tactical guidance is incredibly broad and detailed in scope with no constraints. The guidance has no Parliamentary input in to its drafting. Even qualified by ‘have regard’, Cl147 is interference in how OFCOM carries out regulation. Cl147 should be deleted. Detailed Amendment Cl 147 Delete 3 - Clause 146 Directions in Special Circumstances Cl146 provides strong powers for the Secretary of State to direct OFCOM to intervene withplatforms if the SoS ‘ has reasonable grounds for believing that circumstances exist that present a threat (a) to the health or safety of the public, or (b) to national security. ’ There is no parliamentary scrutiny of the power. The SoS must publish its reasoning unless national security is involved. There is no urgency threshold or requirement in the Clause. The Secretary of State is not required to take advice from an expert body such as the NPCC or PHE or the NSA/NSC in assessing ‘reasonable grounds’ for action. The power is not bounded by the Bill’s definition of harm. The instructions do two things. Firstly they direct OFCOM to use their (quite weak) media literacy duties to respond to the circumstances. Secondly a direction turns on a power for OFCOM to ask a platform to produce a public statement about what the platform is doing to counter the circumstances/threats in the direction order (similar insomeways to the treatment of harm to adults). This is trying to shame a company in to doing something without making them do it.
chunks.display_text: a code of practice’ CL 40(1) delete after ‘may’ to end. Insert after ‘may’: ‘on receipt of a code write with in one month of that day to OFCOM with reasoned, evidence-based suggestions for modifying the code. (a) OFCOM shall have due regard to the Secretary of State’s letter. OFCOM shall reply to the Secretary of State with in one month of receipt. (b) The Secretary of State may only write to OFCOM twice under this Section foreachcode. (c) The Secretary of State and OFCOM shall publish their letters as soon as reasonable possible after transmission, having made any reasonable redactions for public safety and national security. Cl 40(2)- (12) delete Cl 41 title of Clause – replace ‘direction’ with ‘changes arising from correspondence under Section 40’ 41(1) delete 41(2) replace text after ‘following a’ with ‘changes to a code of practice arising from correspondence under Section 40, the affirmative procedure applies’. 41(3) delete 41(5)-(7) delete Note that, based on our advice, the Shadow Digital Minister tabled a similar amendment (NC12) which was debated by the Committee but rejected by the Government on 14 th June . 2 – Clause 147 Secretary of State’s Guidance The Secretary of State takes powers in Cl147 to issue tactical guidance to OFCOM on the ‘exercise of their functions’, to which OFCOM should have regard. This is in addition to Cl 143 which allows the SoS to make a statement of strategic priorities relating to online safety. The tactical guidance is incredibly broad and detailed in scope with no constraints. The guidance has no Parliamentary input in to its drafting. Even qualified by ‘have regard’, Cl147 is interference in how OFCOM carries out regulation. Cl147 should be deleted. Detailed Amendment Cl 147 Delete 3 - Clause 146 Directions in Special Circumstances Cl146 provides strong powers for the Secretary of State to direct OFCOM to intervene withplatforms if the SoS ‘ has reasonable grounds for believing that circumstances exist that present a threat (a) to the health or safety of the public, or (b) to national security. ’ There is no parliamentary scrutiny of the power. The SoS must publish its reasoning unless national security is involved. There is no urgency threshold or requirement in the Clause. The Secretary of State is not required to take advice from an expert body such as the NPCC or PHE or the NSA/NSC in assessing ‘reasonable grounds’ for action. The power is not bounded by the Bill’s definition of harm. The instructions do two things. Firstly they direct OFCOM to use their (quite weak) media literacy duties to respond to the circumstances. Secondly a direction turns on a power for OFCOM to ask a platform to produce a public statement about what the platform is doing to counter the circumstances/threats in the direction order (similar insomeways to the treatment of harm to adults). This is trying to shame a company in to doing something without making them do it.

chunks.header: Clause 40 amend title to –‘Secretary of State’s powers to suggest modification to (Part 2/2)
chunks.text: for OFCOM to ask a platform to produce a public statement about what the platform is doing to counter the circumstances/threats in the direction order (similar insomeways to the treatment of harm to adults). This is trying to shame a company in to doing something without making them do it. This power allows the Secretary of State directly to target a given company – there is potential for misuse of such an ability. The Explanatory Notes say ‘ the Secretary of State could issue a direction during a pandemic to require OFCOM to; give priority to ensuring that health misin for mation and disin for mation is effectively tackled when exercising its media literacy function; and to require service providers to report on the action they are taking to address this issue. Recent experience of the COVID pandemic and the Russian invasion of Ukraine suggests that the government can easily legislate when required in an emergency, including recalling parliament. This is a strong power, cutting through regulatory independence, targeting individual companies to evoke a quite weak effect. It isn’t being justified as an ‘emergency’ power where theneedtomove swiftly is paramount. The outcome (which might be useful) could be a chieved by the Secretary of State writing to bring a matter to OFCOM’s attention for which OFCOM has due regard. If a heavier-duty version is required in a crisis then the government can legislate for that and explain to Parliament why the power is required in the context of the crisis. Amendment The amendment provides for the Secretary of State to write to OFCOM to bring a matter to their attention, OFCOM tohavedue regard to the letter and leaving OFCOM to decide whether to apply the powers. Detailed amendment
chunks.display_text: This power allows the Secretary of State directly to target a given company – there is potential for misuse of such an ability. The Explanatory Notes say ‘ the Secretary of State could issue a direction during a pandemic to require OFCOM to; give priority to ensuring that health misin for mation and disin for mation is effectively tackled when exercising its media literacy function; and to require service providers to report on the action they are taking to address this issue. Recent experience of the COVID pandemic and the Russian invasion of Ukraine suggests that the government can easily legislate when required in an emergency, including recalling parliament. This is a strong power, cutting through regulatory independence, targeting individual companies to evoke a quite weak effect. It isn’t being justified as an ‘emergency’ power where theneedtomove swiftly is paramount. The outcome (which might be useful) could be a chieved by the Secretary of State writing to bring a matter to OFCOM’s attention for which OFCOM has due regard. If a heavier-duty version is required in a crisis then the government can legislate for that and explain to Parliament why the power is required in the context of the crisis. Amendment The amendment provides for the Secretary of State to write to OFCOM to bring a matter to their attention, OFCOM tohavedue regard to the letter and leaving OFCOM to decide whether to apply the powers. Detailed amendment

chunks.text: 146(1) replace ‘direction’ with ‘write’ New sub clause 1(A) OFCOM should have due regard to a letter from the Secretary of State sent under this Section. OFCOM should publish its response to the Secretary of State as soon as is reasonably possible, redacted appropriately for national security matters. (2) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ throughout ‘requiring’ with ‘asking’ Replace ‘give’ with ‘to consider giving’ (3) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ throughout Replace ‘requiring’ with ‘asking’ (6) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ addtoend of sentence ‘as soon as reasonably possible’ (7) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ (8) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ (10) replace ‘direction’ with ‘letter’ My collaborator Professor Lorna Woods and I would be delighted to discuss these proposals further, if helpful. I am copying this letter to the Stephen McGinness and Conor Durham. Yours sincerely WILLIAM PERRIN william@cankfarm. com
